name: Littleton
coordsSource: web-verified:GENUKI SU452327;OS-postcode-cluster(St Catherines); two-source(~150m); listing-centroid n=17 ~2.5km SE (parish incl Harestock); 2026-06-04
overview: Littleton is a village in the joint civil parish of Littleton and Harestock (population 3,577 at the 2011 Census), three miles north-west of Winchester city centre on the downs between the B3420 (the old Roman road to Cirencester) and the B3049 Stockbridge road. Half a dozen of its houses date from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries — some are Hampshire Hall houses — clustered around the medieval St Catherine's church and the old parsonage. The bulk of the village dates from the early twentieth century, with a village hall built in 1920 as a memorial to residents killed in the First World War. Littleton holds around 450 homes and 2,800 electors across the joint parish (Harestock to the south-east is the newer settlement). It sits inside the Winchester gap zone and just outside the South Downs National Park to the south.
character: Genuine 'open' village with two centres: an older lane (Main Road and Andover Road) of brick-and-flint and 1920s–1930s cottages around St Catherine's church and the village hall, plus a tighter ribbon of post-war and recent-build housing toward Harestock. Quiet but well-equipped, with extensive sports and recreational facilities.
amenities: Running Horse Inn (Main Road, gastro pub with rooms)
Littleton Recreation Ground, tennis, cricket and football
Harestock garage and small shops on the southern end (1 mi)
Wyke Mark and Andover Road local convenience shops
Sparsholt College Hampshire (3 mi) — land-based further education
Full Winchester high street, Cathedral and Sainsbury's (3 mi)

transport.roads: B3420 (Roman road, parish edge); B3049 (Stockbridge Road, parish edge); A34/M3 J9 ~3 mi

thingsToDo: Walking on the Roman road (B3420) and around Crab Wood SSSI
Hillier Garden Centre, Sir Harold Hillier Gardens (8 mi)
Sparsholt College open days and equestrian centre
Winchester Cathedral and the city Christmas market (3 mi)

placesToEat.name: The Running Horse Inn
placesToEat.notes: Village pub on Main Road; lunch, dinner and rooms

placesToEat.name: Café Monde, Harestock
placesToEat.notes: Local café at the Harestock end of the parish

pros: Good Ofsted primary and Good catchment secondary (Henry Beaufort) inside the parish
Three miles from Winchester station and the direct Waterloo line
Mix of older brick-and-flint and inter-war housing; not pure estate
Active village life — show, hall, recreation ground, multiple groups
Outstanding sixth-form (Peter Symonds) is one bus or a short drive away
cons: SO22 detached stock averages £920k — well above budget; terraced stock pushes £500k
B3420 and B3049 are busy commuter routes at peak
No mainline station within the village — Winchester drive or bus needed
Recent new-build pressure on the Harestock side has shaped local feeling
Some streets feel suburban rather than rural
whoItSuits: Strong fit for a solo first-time buyer who wants Winchester convenience without buying inside the city — Henry Beaufort and Harestock primary are both Good and inside walking distance, and you keep direct trains to Waterloo. Realistically, only terraces and the cheaper end of semis will come into the £400k budget here; everything detached is over £900k. For prettier-but-pricier look at Sparsholt or Crawley (SO21); for cheaper similar commute, South Wonston (SO21) or Sutton Scotney further up the A34.
primarySupermarket: Sainsbury's, Middle Brook Street, Winchester (3.0 mi); Waitrose, Stockbridge Road, Winchester (2.5 mi)
